Auto-generated developer documentation

AutoDocX

Developer-focused documentation generated from project scan data and code structure. Use this page to understand the project, inspect APIs, review modules, and see class/function signatures.

Provider: openai Model: gpt-5-mini Scan: 20260708_080844_17f5780e Understanding: 20260708_080949_c5714944 Created: 2026-07-08T08:09:49.122692+00:00

Overview

This project provides an automated developer-documentation engine (AutoDocX). It scans a local codebase, parses Python source files to extract symbols and FastAPI routes, analyzes module-level structure and dependencies, generates LLM-ready context, asks a configured LLM for a narrative understanding, merges that narrative with machine-extracted reference data, and builds standalone HTML documentation with optional runtime/tooling context (screenshots and notes). The application is exposed as a FastAPI service with endpoints to scan projects, compare saved scans, generate or update HTML documentation, and run LLM-based analyses of both code and runtime context. Storage components persist scans, LLM understandings, and generated documents under a workspace directory configured in app.core.config.
API paths, handlers, signatures, parameters, classes, and methods are taken from parser data where available, not guessed by the LLM.

Quickstart

Start the FastAPI server, then open the generated API docs or call the project documentation sync endpoint.

Run the application PowerShell / terminal 
python -m uvicorn app.main:app --reload
Generate documentation Python request example 
import requests

response = requests.post(
    "http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/projects/documentation/sync",
    json={
        "project_path": r"C:\path\to\AutoDocX"
    },
    timeout=900,
)

response.raise_for_status()
print(response.json())

Project Statistics

Syntax error modules 0

Architecture

The application follows a modular pipeline with clear layers: scanning/parsing, analysis, LLM orchestration, document generation, and storage + HTTP API. The scanner layer (app.services.project_scanner and app.services.code_parser) enumerates files, computes hashes, and extracts AST-level metadata. The analyzer layer (app.services.project_analyzer and app.services.project_context_builder) builds cross-file relationships, API reference, module references and compact context for the LLM. The LLM layer (app.services.llm.* and app.services.project_understanding_service / app.services.runtime_context_analyzer) picks a provider via a factory, prepares prompts (app.services.llm.prompts), and generates structured JSON understanding (with optional image inputs). The document layer (app.services.document_builder and app.services.document_storage) renders standalone HTML and persists assets. The FastAPI layer (app.api.project_routes and app.main) exposes endpoints that orchestrate the pipeline and call storage services (scan_storage, understanding_storage, document_storage). Configuration and workspace paths are provided by app.core.config.

Runtime and Tooling Context

Runtime summary

This repository is a Python-based documentation-generation service composed of (1) a FastAPI backend that scans local Python projects, generates a structured "understanding" using an LLM, compares scans, and builds/stores standalone HTML developer documentation; and (2) a Streamlit UI that calls the backend to trigger scans and to attach runtime/tooling context (text blocks + screenshots). The backend uses local filesystem workspace paths to save scans, understandings and generated documents; it supports multiple LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic/Claude, etc.) via a provider factory. Screenshots provided show the Streamlit app running on port 8501 and the backend producing HTTP request logs for /api/projects/... endpoints.

Tooling stack

Python

Primary implementation language for backend and UI.

Evidence: Project files are Python modules (app/*, ui/streamlit_app.py). Code-scan statistics show many python modules, classes and functions.

FastAPI

HTTP API backend exposing project scan, compare, understand, and document-generation endpoints.

Evidence: app/api/project_routes.py contains many router.post/get endpoints (e.g. POST /api/projects/documentation/sync, /scan, /{project_name}/understand). app.main defines routes '/' and '/health'.

Uvicorn (ASGI server)

Runs the FastAPI application in development or production.

Evidence: Terminal screenshot shows 'Application startup complete.' followed by many INFO lines of HTTP requests (typical Uvicorn access logs).

Streamlit

Frontend UI used by developers to trigger documentation sync, attach runtime context blocks, and upload screenshots.

Evidence: ui/streamlit_app.py module present; terminal screenshot shows 'streamlit run ui/streamlit_app.py' and the Streamlit server messages including 'Local URL: http://localhost:8501'.

requests (HTTP client)

Streamlit app uses requests to call FastAPI endpoints (sync and sync-with-context).

Evidence: ui.streamlit_app functions call requests.post and requests.get (parse_json_response, sync_documentation, sync_documentation_with_context).

Local filesystem workspace

Stores scan JSON, understandings, generated HTML, and runtime assets/screenshots.

Evidence: Documented storage paths in services: workspace/scans/<project_name>/<scan_id>.json and workspace/understandings/<project_name>/<understanding_id>.json; DocumentStorage docstring mentions storing HTML and runtime assets.

LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic/Claude, Claude via 'anthropic')

Generate narrative project 'understanding' and analyze runtime/screenshot context with vision-capable LLMs.

Evidence: app.services.llm.openai_provider, app.services.llm.anthropic_provider, app.services.llm.factory, and usage in project_routes endpoints (get_llm_provider, get_llm_api_key, get_llm_model, project_understanding_service.generate_understanding, runtime_context_analyzer).

Runtime flow

1

User triggers documentation sync from the UI

Developer opens the Streamlit UI and calls the synchronization action (either the all-in-one documentation/sync endpoint or the sync-with-context endpoint that accepts context blocks and screenshots). The Streamlit UI builds a multipart form payload (if screenshots present) and posts to the FastAPI backend.

Evidence: ui/streamlit_app.py contains sync_documentation and sync_documentation_with_context functions that POST to /api/projects/documentation/sync and /api/projects/documentation/sync-with-context. Terminal screenshot 2 shows 'streamlit run ui/streamlit_app.py' and Streamlit accessible at localhost:8501.

2

Backend scans the local project

The FastAPI endpoint calls ProjectScanner.scan_project which lists files, parses Python modules via PythonCodeParser, computes hashes and project statistics, and returns a stored scan object.

Evidence: app.api.project_routes sync endpoints call project_scanner.scan_project; app.services.project_scanner and app.services.code_parser exist with docstrings describing scanning/parsing behavior. project_routes called_functions include project_scanner.scan_project and scan_storage.save_scan.

3

Scan is saved to scan storage (workspace)

The scan result is saved to local ScanStorage under workspace/scans/<project_name>/<scan_id>.json and the endpoint records the scan metadata.

Evidence: app.services.scan_storage.ScanStorage docstring describes save location. project_routes code calls scan_storage.save_scan(scan_result=scan_result).

4

If a previous document exists: compare scans

If a latest document exists for the project, the backend recovers previous runtime assets (screenshots) if necessary, obtains the previous runtime context, and uses ScanComparator to compare the old and new scans to determine if documentation-relevant changes exist.

Evidence: project_routes uses document_storage.list_documents, _recover_runtime_assets_for_document, _get_runtime_context_from_document, and scan_comparator.compare_scans. app.services.scan_comparator exists and project_routes calls comparison_result.get('summary', {}).

5

Generate LLM 'understanding' and analyze runtime context

When changes exist (or for initial creation), the backend builds an LLM-ready context and calls the configured LLM provider via ProjectUnderstandingService.generate_understanding; separately, RuntimeContextAnalyzer may analyze supplied screenshots and user-written blocks, producing structured runtime context that gets merged into the document metadata.

Evidence: project_routes calls _generate_and_save_understanding (calls project_understanding_service.generate_understanding via get_llm_provider/get_llm_api_key/get_llm_model). RuntimeContextAnalyzer class is present, and project_routes calls _analyze_runtime_context_with_llm(stored_scan=..., runtime_context=...).

6

Build HTML documentation and save document artifact

DocumentBuilder builds a standalone HTML developer documentation page from the stored understanding and the runtime context; DocumentStorage saves the HTML and associated metadata (including runtime context and asset references).

Evidence: project_routes calls DocumentBuilder.build_html and document_storage.save_document. app.services.document_builder.DocumentBuilder and app.services.document_storage.DocumentStorage exist with docstrings describing HTML generation and storage of runtime assets.

7

Streamlit UI opens generated HTML or returns metadata

After a successful sync, the backend returns a DocumentationSyncResponse with document_id and metadata. The Streamlit UI uses build_document_url to open the /api/projects/{project_name}/documents/{document_id}/html endpoint (FileResponse) or display operation results in the UI.

Evidence: ui.streamlit_app.build_document_url builds a URL using normalize_api_url and the API path; project_routes has GET /{project_name}/documents/{document_id}/html which returns FileResponse built from document_storage.get_document_file.

8

Runtime assets handling and merging

When the user supplies runtime context blocks and screenshots, the backend saves uploaded screenshot files to a workspace assets batch, builds a runtime_context object linking blocks to screenshot assets using screenshot_index, and merges with any existing runtime context from previous documents (deduping screenshots and blocks).

Evidence: project_routes.sync_project_documentation_with_context calls _save_uploaded_screenshots_to_temp, document_storage.prepare_runtime_assets, _build_runtime_context_from_blocks. There are helper functions in project_routes for _merge_runtime_contexts, _merge_screenshot_lists, _merge_context_blocks, and signatures/deduplication functions such as _screenshot_signature and _context_block_signature.

Screenshot insights

Backend access logs showing API activity

What it shows: Terminal shows Uvicorn/ASGI access logs with repeated GET requests to /api/projects/AutoDocX/documents and a POST to /api/projects/documentation/sync returning '200 OK'. Also shows 'Application startup complete.'

Why it matters: Confirms the FastAPI backend is running and serving the documented API endpoints; demonstrates the Streamlit UI (or another client) is successfully calling the backend to list documents and to trigger documentation synchronization.

Streamlit UI startup and port

What it shows: Terminal shows the exact command used to run the UI: 'streamlit run ui/streamlit_app.py' and the Streamlit server messages including 'Local URL: http://localhost:8501' and 'Network URL: http://192.168.43.184:8501'.

Why it matters: Confirms the repository includes a Streamlit app and how developers run it. The UI serves on port 8501 and will call the backend endpoints (so developer must ensure backend is running and that the API base URL configured in the UI points to the FastAPI server).

Operational notes

  • Confirmed: The Streamlit frontend is run with 'streamlit run ui/streamlit_app.py' (screenshot shows this command and the Streamlit server on port 8501).
  • Confirmed: The backend exposes many project-related REST endpoints (see app/api/project_routes.py), including POST /api/projects/documentation/sync and POST /api/projects/documentation/sync-with-context which accept uploaded screenshots and ordered runtime context blocks.
  • Not visible from the provided context: The exact command used to start the FastAPI backend process. Typical invocation is 'uvicorn app.main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000' but that exact command is not present in the scanned files or screenshots.
  • Required environment variables: LLM_PROVIDER and provider-specific API keys are required (app.core.config.get_llm_provider, get_llm_api_key, get_llm_model). The repository expects these to be set in the environment or a .env file.
  • Workspace directories are created by app.core.config.create_required_directories and the storage implementation expects write access to these workspace paths (scans, understandings, documents).
  • Streamlit build_context_blocks_payload uses screenshot_index to match uploaded files to context blocks. The provided user payload metadata shows two context blocks and two uploaded PNG images (screenshot_001.png, screenshot_002.png).

Runtime risks and gaps

medium

Backend start command not documented in repo scan

The code scan shows FastAPI app definitions and Uvicorn logs were observed in the screenshot, but there is no explicit top-level run script or README entry extracted by the scanner that documents how to start the backend. A new developer may not know which host/port or launch options to use.

high

Missing or undocumented environment variable requirements (LLM keys, provider selection)

LLM_PROVIDER and provider-specific environment variables are validated in app.core.config.get_llm_provider/get_llm_api_key/get_llm_model and will raise ValueError if missing or invalid. The necessary variables and example .env are not visible in the provided context.

medium

Runtime storage is filesystem-only and needs directory permissions

Document, scan, and understanding storage all use local workspace paths. If the workspace path is not writable or not created, runtime errors can occur. create_required_directories exists but it must be invoked before operations or the server/startup path must call it.

high

Potential privacy/security risk from screenshot uploads

Screenshots uploaded via sync-with-context can contain sensitive PII. There is no evidence of sanitization, redaction, or access controls in the provided scan; stored runtime assets may be accessible from generated HTML or filesystem if not protected.

medium

LLM calls may be rate-limited or incur costs

ProjectUnderstandingService and RuntimeContextAnalyzer call external LLM providers. Without quota/keys control, generation may fail or incur unexpected costs. Also error handling maps provider errors to HTTP 502; integration tests/configuration are advisable.

medium

Unclear default backend port and CORS/origin setup for Streamlit integration

Streamlit UI runs on port 8501, but the backend API base URL used by the UI is built at runtime (normalize_api_url). The default API host/port expected by the UI is not visible; CORS configuration (to allow the UI to call the API from browser) is not shown in the excerpt.

high

No visible authentication/authorization

API endpoints appear to accept uploads and trigger LLM operations; there is no evidence in the scanned routes of authentication or fine-grained authorization. If deployed beyond a trusted local environment, this is a security gap.

Supporting screenshots

These screenshots were analyzed together with the project scan and user notes to generate the runtime understanding above.

/api/projects/documentation/sync-with-context

Handler signature

def sync_project_documentation_with_context(project_path: str = Form(..., min_length=1, description='Absolute path of the project whose documentation should be created or updated'), additional_context: str = Form(default='', description='Optional general project context that is not visible from source code.'), context_blocks_json: str = Form(default='[]', description='JSON list of ordered runtime context blocks. Request example

Example requests 
import requests

    data = {
        "project_path": r"C:\path\to\project",
        "additional_context": "",
        "context_blocks_json": "[]",
    }

    files = []

    response = requests.post(
        "http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/projects/documentation/sync-with-context",
        data=data,
        files=files,
        timeout=900,
    )

    response.raise_for_status()
    print(response.json())

import requests

    response = requests.post(
        "http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/projects/AutoDocX/understand",
        json={
        "scan_id": "scan_id"
    },
        timeout=900,
    )

    response.raise_for_status()
    print(response.json())

Usage pattern

Import example Python 
from app.api.project_routes import build_project_context

# Use build_project_context based on the module API.

Functions

_parse_context_blocks_json

def _parse_context_blocks_json(context_blocks_json: str) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:

Parse ordered runtime context blocks sent by the Streamlit UI. Rule: • If title, text, and screenshot_index are all empty, ignore the block. • If any one exists, keep the block.

Calls made by this function

Called function Call details Why it matters

ValueError ValueError('context_blocks_json must be a JSON list.') Calls ValueError, which appears to support the ValueError step in this function.
str.strip str.strip(...) Calls str.strip, which appears to support the strip step in this function.
item.get item.get('screenshot_index') Calls item.get, which appears to support the get step in this function.
bool bool(title) Calls bool, which appears to support the bool step in this function.
context_blocks.append bool(text) Calls context_blocks.append, which appears to support the append step in this function.
str context_blocks.append({'title': title, 'text': text, 'screenshot_index': screenshot_index}) Calls str, which appears to support the str step in this function.
int ValueError('context_blocks_json must be valid JSON.') Calls int, which appears to support the int step in this function.

_build_runtime_context_from_blocks

def _build_runtime_context_from_blocks(additional_context: str, context_blocks_json: str, runtime_assets: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:

Build the final runtime context object used by DocumentBuilder and DocumentStorage. This connects each context block to the correct uploaded/pasted screenshot using screenshot_index.

Calls made by this function

_merge_runtime_contexts

def _merge_runtime_contexts(previous_runtime_context: dict[str, Any] | None, new_runtime_context: dict[str, Any] | None) -> dict[str, Any]:

Merge old runtime context with the newly submitted runtime context. This allows every new document version to carry forward old screenshots/text and add new ones.

Calls made by this function

Called function Call details Why it matters
_merge_additional_context _merge_additional_context(previous_value=previous_context.get('additional_context'), new_value=current_context.get('additional_context')) Calls _merge_additional_context, which appears to support the merge additional context step in this function.

_screenshot_signature

def _screenshot_signature(screenshot: dict[str, Any]) -> str:

Build a stable signature for deduping screenshots. asset_file/html_src is more reliable than screenshot_id because each upload batch can reuse screenshot_001.

Calls made by this function

sync_project_documentation

def sync_project_documentation(request: DocumentationSyncRequest) -> DocumentationSyncResponse:

Complete documentation workflow. The endpoint: 1. Scans and saves the project. 2. Finds the latest document. 3. Creates initial documentation when no document exists. 4. Compares scans when a document exists. 5. Creates a new version when changes exist. 6. Returns the existing document when there are no changes.

Calls made by this function

Called function Call details Why it matters
router.post router.post('/documentation/sync', response_model=DocumentationSyncResponse, status_code=status.HTTP_200_OK, summary='Create or update project documentation using one request') Calls router.post, which appears to support the post step in this function.

scan_storage.save_scan scan_storage.save_scan(scan_result=scan_result) Writes or persists output produced by the workflow.
str.strip str.strip(...) Calls str.strip, which appears to support the strip step in this function.

sync_project_documentation_with_context

def sync_project_documentation_with_context(project_path: str = Form(..., min_length=1, description='Absolute path of the project whose documentation should be created or updated'), additional_context: str = Form(default='', description='Optional general project context that is not visible from source code.'), context_blocks_json: str = Form(default='[]', description='JSON list of ordered runtime context blocks. Each block can contain title, text, and screenshot_index.'), screenshots: list[UploadFile] | None = File(default=None, description='Optional screenshots from Docker, Temporal, Swagger, Cortex, ServiceNow, dashboards, logs, or other project tooling.')) -> DocumentationSyncResponse:

Complete documentation workflow with external runtime/tooling context. This endpoint keeps the original code documentation pipeline but also accepts human-written context and screenshots. The extra context is added to the final HTML documentation under Runtime and Tooling Context.

update_project_document

def update_project_document(project_name: str, request: DocumentUpdateRequest) -> DocumentUpdateResponse:

Update an existing generated document using a new saved scan. Important: • If the previous document contains runtime/tooling context, it is carried into the new document. • This prevents screenshots, notes, and runtime understanding from disappearing during manual document updates.

Calls made by this function

Called function Call details Why it matters

Constants

Name Type Value
PROJECT_ROOT - Path(__file__).resolve().parents[2]
ENV_FILE_PATH - PROJECT_ROOT / '.env'
WORKSPACE_PATH - PROJECT_ROOT / 'workspace'
SCANS_PATH - WORKSPACE_PATH / 'scans'
UNDERSTANDINGS_PATH - WORKSPACE_PATH / 'understandings'
DOCUMENTS_PATH - WORKSPACE_PATH / 'documents'

app.services.code_parser

app/services/code_parser.py

`app.services.code_parser` contains 1 class(es) 0 sync function(s) 0 async function(s) 0 constant(s) 0 API route(s) and 0 internal dependency link(s).

Public symbols

PythonCodeParser

Usage pattern

Import example Python 
from app.services.code_parser import PythonCodeParser

# Use PythonCodeParser based on the module API.

Classes

PythonCodeParser

class PythonCodeParser:

Reads a Python file and extracts developer-documentation-ready structure from it. Extracted information: • imports • module constants • functions • async functions • classes • class attributes • class methods • function/method signatures • arguments with annotations/defaults • return annotations • docstrings • decorators • FastAPI routes • source previews • content hashes • syntax errors

Attributes

Name Type Default / Value
HTTP_METHODS - {'get', 'post', 'put', 'patch', 'delete', 'options', 'head', 'trace', 'websocket'}

Methods

parse_file

def parse_file(self, project_root: Path, relative_file_path: str) -> dict[str, Any]:

Implements the parse file operation for this module. It coordinates the workflow by calling source_code.splitlines. The call is made as source_code.splitlines(...). It returns dict[str, Any] used by the surrounding workflow.

_extract_import

def _extract_import(node: ast.Import) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:

Implements the extract import operation for this module. It coordinates the workflow by calling imports.append. The call is made as imports.append({'type': 'import', 'module': imported_name.name, 'name': None, 'alias': imported_name.asname, 'line': getattr(node, 'lineno', None), 'statement': f'import {imported_name.name}' if imported_name.asname is None else f'import {imported_name.name} as {imported_name.asname}'}). It returns list[dict[str, Any]] used by the surrounding workflow.

Calls made by this function

_calculate_node_hash

def _calculate_node_hash(node: ast.AST) -> str:

Creates a stable SHA-256 hash from an AST node. Formatting-only changes generally do not change this hash.

app.services.document_builder

app/services/document_builder.py

`app.services.document_builder` contains 1 class(es) 0 sync function(s) 0 async function(s) 0 constant(s) 0 API route(s) and 0 internal dependency link(s).

Public symbols

DocumentBuilder

Usage pattern

Import example Python 
from app.services.document_builder import DocumentBuilder

# Use DocumentBuilder based on the module API.

Classes

DocumentBuilder

class DocumentBuilder:

Builds a standalone professional developer documentation HTML from a saved project understanding. The output is closer to developer documentation pages: • left navigation • main article content • right table of contents • API reference cards • parameter tables • module reference cards • class/function/method signatures • code examples • clean callouts

Methods

build_html

app.services.document_storage

app/services/document_storage.py

`app.services.document_storage` contains 1 class(es) 0 sync function(s) 0 async function(s) 0 constant(s) 0 API route(s) and 1 internal dependency link(s).

Public symbols

DocumentStorage

Usage pattern

Import example Python 
from app.services.document_storage import DocumentStorage

# Use DocumentStorage based on the module API.

Classes

DocumentStorage

class DocumentStorage:

Stores generated HTML documentation files, document metadata, and optional runtime context assets such as uploaded screenshots.

Attributes

Name Type Default / Value
ALLOWED_SCREENSHOT_EXTENSIONS - {'.png', '.jpg', '.jpeg', '.webp'}

Methods

save_document

def save_document(self, project_name: str, understanding_id: str, scan_id: str, html_content: str, previous_document_id: str | None = None, update_type: str = 'initial', comparison_summary: dict[str, Any] | None = None, runtime_context: dict[str, Any] | None = None) -> dict[str, Any]:

Save one generated HTML document and its metadata. runtime_context is optional and is used for extra project information that is not visible from code alone, such as Docker, Temporal, Cortex, ServiceNow, screenshots, dashboards, or deployment notes.

prepare_runtime_assets

def prepare_runtime_assets(self, project_name: str, files: list[dict[str, Any]] | None = None) -> dict[str, Any]:

Save uploaded screenshot files under the project document assets directory. Expected file item format: { "filename": "docker.png", "content_type": "image/png", "file_path": Path("temporary_upload.png") } This method is intentionally framework-neutral. FastAPI routes can first save UploadFile objects to temporary files, then pass those paths here.

recover_missing_runtime_assets_from_html

def recover_missing_runtime_assets_from_html(self, project_name: str, document_metadata: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:

Recover missing screenshot asset files from the standalone generated HTML document. Why this exists: • Shared HTML files can contain screenshots as base64 data:image/... values. • A new developer may not have the original workspace/assets folder. • Before reusing old runtime_context for LLM analysis, we recreate missing image files from the embedded base64 images. This method returns updated document metadata. It also writes the repaired metadata back to disk when metadata_file exists.

Calls made by this function

Called function Call details Why it matters

_extract_base64_screenshots_from_html

def _extract_base64_screenshots_from_html(html_file: Path) -> list[str]:

Extract embedded screenshot data URIs from the Supporting screenshots section. Prefer screenshot-card images so we do not accidentally extract unrelated embedded icons.

generate_json

def generate_json(self, system_prompt: str, user_prompt: str) -> dict[str, Any]:

Implements the generate json operation for this module. It coordinates the workflow by calling self.client.messages.create. The call is made as self.client.messages.create(model=self.model, max_tokens=8000, temperature=0, system=system_prompt, messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': user_prompt}]). It returns dict[str, Any] used by the surrounding workflow.

from app.services.llm.factory import LLMProviderFactory

Classes

LLMProviderFactory

class LLMProviderFactory:

Creates an LLM provider from the provider name supplied in the API request.

Attributes

Name Type Default / Value
PROVIDER_ALIASES - {'openai': 'openai', 'open_ai': 'openai', 'anthropic': 'anthropic', 'claude': 'anthropic'}

Methods

create_provider

def create_provider(cls, provider_name: str, api_key: str, model: str | None = None) -> BaseLLMProvider:

Implements the create provider operation for this module. It coordinates the workflow by calling cls.normalize_provider_name. The call is made as cls.normalize_provider_name(provider_name=provider_name). It returns BaseLLMProvider used by the surrounding workflow.

normalize_provider_name

def normalize_provider_name(cls, provider_name: str) -> str:

Implements the normalize provider name operation for this module. It coordinates the workflow by calling provider_name.strip.lower. The call is made as provider_name.strip.lower(...). It returns str used by the surrounding workflow.

generate_json

def generate_json(self, system_prompt: str, user_prompt: str) -> dict[str, Any]:

Implements the generate json operation for this module. It coordinates the workflow by calling self.client.responses.create. The call is made as self.client.responses.create(model=self.model, instructions=system_prompt, input=user_prompt, max_output_tokens=8000). It returns dict[str, Any] used by the surrounding workflow.

app.services.llm.prompts

app/services/llm/prompts.py

`app.services.llm.prompts` contains 0 class(es) 1 sync function(s) 0 async function(s) 1 constant(s) 0 API route(s) and 0 internal dependency link(s).

Public symbols

PROJECT_UNDERSTANDING_SYSTEM_PROMPTbuild_project_understanding_prompt

Usage pattern

Import example Python 
from app.services.llm.prompts import PROJECT_UNDERSTANDING_SYSTEM_PROMPT

# Use PROJECT_UNDERSTANDING_SYSTEM_PROMPT based on the module API.

Constants

Name Type Value
PROJECT_UNDERSTANDING_SYSTEM_PROMPT - '\nYou are AutoDocX, a professional developer documentation engine.\n\nYou receive deterministic project context extracted from source code.\nThe context may include modules, classes, functions, method signatures,\nFastAPI routes, dependencies, docstrings, and project statistics.\n\nYour job is to generate clean developer documentation metadata.\n\nImportant rules:\n\n1. Do not invent files, functions, classes, APIs, databases, services,\n dependencies, parameters, return values, or behavior.\n2. Keep module names, function names, class names, method names, and API\n paths exactly as supplied.\n3. Write like professional developer docs, not like an audit report.\n4. Do not write phrases like "Confirmed", "Reasonable inference",\n "Based on the context", or "It appears".\n5. If something is unclear, write a short neutral sentence such as:\n "The provided scan does not expose this detail."\n6. Do not include Markdown code fences.\n7. Do not include API keys, credentials, secrets, or environment values.\n8. Return exactly one valid JSON object.\n9. Do not return text before or after the JSON object.\n\nThe JSON object must follow this exact structure:\n\n{\n "project_summary": "string",\n "architecture_overview": "string",\n "execution_flow": [\n {\n "step": 1,\n "title": "string",\n "description": "string",\n "related_modules": ["string"]\n }\n ],\n "module_responsibilities": [\n {\n "module": "string",\n "file": "string",\n "responsibility": "string",\n "important_symbols": ["string"]\n }\n ],\n "api_overview": [\n {\n "method": "string",\n "path": "string",\n "handler": "string",\n "purpose": "string"\n }\n ],\n "key_dependencies": [\n {\n "source": "string",\n "target": "string",\n "purpose": "string"\n }\n ],\n "risks_and_gaps": [\n {\n "title": "string",\n "description": "string",\n "severity": "low | medium | high"\n }\n ],\n "recommended_document_sections": [\n {\n "title": "string",\n "purpose": "string"\n }\n ]\n}\n\nStyle requirements:\n\n- project_summary should be 1 to 2 concise paragraphs.\n- architecture_overview should explain the application layers and how\n code moves through them.\n- execution_flow should describe runtime flow in practical developer terms.\n- module_responsibilities should be short and specific.\n- api_overview should explain what each endpoint is used for.\n- risks_and_gaps should focus on real engineering gaps such as\n authentication, error handling, retries, persistence, testing,\n configuration, observability, and deployment readiness.\n- recommended_document_sections should suggest useful developer docs,\n not generic business report sections.\n'.strip()

Functions

build_project_understanding_prompt

def build_project_understanding_prompt(project_context: dict[str, Any]) -> str:

Implements the build project understanding prompt operation for this module. It coordinates the workflow by calling json.dumps. The call is made as json.dumps(project_context, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False). It returns str used by the surrounding workflow.

app.services.project_analyzer

app/services/project_analyzer.py

`app.services.project_analyzer` contains 1 class(es) 0 sync function(s) 0 async function(s) 0 constant(s) 0 API route(s) and 0 internal dependency link(s).

Public symbols

ProjectAnalyzer

Usage pattern

Import example Python 
from app.services.project_analyzer import ProjectAnalyzer

# Use ProjectAnalyzer based on the module API.

Classes

ProjectAnalyzer

class ProjectAnalyzer:

Builds project-level relationships from parsed Python files. The analyzer: • maps project modules • detects internal module dependencies • collects functions, async functions, classes, methods and constants • preserves signatures, arguments, returns, docstrings and source previews • discovers FastAPI router prefixes • discovers include_router relationships • builds complete FastAPI endpoint paths • creates developer documentation references • creates project-level statistics

Methods

analyze_project

def analyze_project(self, project_root: Path, parsed_files: list[dict[str, Any]]) -> dict[str, Any]:

Implements the analyze project operation for this module. It coordinates the workflow by calling self._build_module_map. The call is made as self._build_module_map(parsed_files=parsed_files). It returns dict[str, Any] used by the surrounding workflow.

app.services.project_context_builder

app/services/project_context_builder.py

`app.services.project_context_builder` contains 1 class(es) 0 sync function(s) 0 async function(s) 0 constant(s) 0 API route(s) and 0 internal dependency link(s).

Public symbols

ProjectContextBuilder

Usage pattern

Import example Python 
from app.services.project_context_builder import ProjectContextBuilder

# Use ProjectContextBuilder based on the module API.

Classes

ProjectContextBuilder

class ProjectContextBuilder:

Converts a complete AutoDocX scan into structured project context. Supported modes: detailed Keeps complete module, symbol, route, import, dependency, source preview, signature, and content-hash information. llm Produces a smaller context intended for LLM documentation generation while still preserving developer-documentation fields such as signatures, arguments, return types, routes, class methods, and docstrings.

Attributes

Name Type Default / Value
SUPPORTED_MODES - {'detailed', 'llm'}
ENTRY_POINT_NAMES - {'main.py', 'app.py', 'server.py', 'manage.py', 'cli.py', '__main__.py'}
CONFIG_FILE_NAMES - {'requirements.txt', 'pyproject.toml', 'setup.py', 'setup.cfg', 'tox.ini', 'pytest.ini', 'dockerfile', 'docker-compose.yml', 'docker-compose.yaml', '.env.example', '.gitignore', 'package.json', 'tsconfig.json', 'alembic.ini'}
DOCUMENTATION_FILE_NAMES - {'readme.md', 'readme.txt', 'contributing.md', 'changelog.md', 'license', 'license.txt'}
SCRIPT_EXTENSIONS - {'.bat', '.ps1', '.sh', '.cmd'}
STANDARD_LIBRARY_MODULES - {'abc', 'ast', 'asyncio', 'collections', 'datetime', 'enum', 'functools', 'hashlib', 'html', 'inspect', 'io', 'json', 'logging', 'os', 'pathlib', 're', 'shutil', 'subprocess', 'sys', 'time', 'typing', 'uuid'}

Methods

_optimize_for_llm

def _optimize_for_llm(self, context: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:

Build LLM context without duplicate top-level data. Goal: • Keep full module reference details so the generated documentation can still show modules, classes, functions, methods, signatures, arguments, return types, docstrings, decorators, and called functions. • Remove duplicate sections that contain the same information and make the LLM prompt too large. • Keep api_reference instead of api_routes because api_reference is the cleaner final documentation source. • Keep module_references instead of modules because module_references is the cleaner final documentation source for the Python Module Reference section. • Remove symbols from LLM context because symbols repeats functions, classes, methods, and constants already present in module_references.

Calls made by this function

Called function Call details Why it matters
context.get context.get('module_references', []) Calls context.get, which appears to support the get step in this function.
self._remove_empty_values context.get('api_reference', []) Calls self._remove_empty_values, which appears to support the remove empty values step in this function.

optimized_module_references.append self._is_empty_package_module(module=module) Calls optimized_module_references.append, which appears to support the append step in this function.
dict optimized_module_references.append(self._remove_empty_values(module)) Calls dict, which appears to support the dict step in this function.

app.services.project_scanner

app/services/project_scanner.py

`app.services.project_scanner` contains 1 class(es) 0 sync function(s) 0 async function(s) 0 constant(s) 0 API route(s) and 2 internal dependency link(s).

Public symbols

ProjectScanner

Usage pattern

Import example Python 
from app.services.project_scanner import ProjectScanner

# Use ProjectScanner based on the module API.

Classes

ProjectScanner

class ProjectScanner:

Scans and analyzes a local project directory. The scanner: • ignores unnecessary folders and files • ignores generated documentation/workspace output • ignores large/binary files that should not go to LLM • lists project files • counts directories • counts file extensions • calculates project size • calculates SHA-256 file hashes • parses Python source files • analyzes project-level relationships

Attributes

Name Type Default / Value
MAX_SCANNED_FILE_SIZE_BYTES - 1000000
IGNORED_DIRECTORIES - {'.git', '.idea', '.vscode', '.venv', 'venv', 'env', '__pycache__', '.pytest_cache', '.mypy_cache', '.ruff_cache', 'node_modules', 'dist', 'build', 'coverage', 'htmlcov', '.tox', '.next', 'workspace', 'assets'}
IGNORED_FILES - {'.DS_Store', 'Thumbs.db'}
IGNORED_EXTENSIONS - {'.pyc', '.pyo', '.pyd', '.log', '.tmp', '.temp', '.swp', '.swo', '.html', '.htm', '.png', '.jpg', '.jpeg', '.webp', '.gif', '.svg', '.ico', '.pdf', '.docx', '.pptx', '.xlsx', '.zip', '.tar', '.gz', '.7z', '.rar', '.exe', '.dll', '.bin', '.db', '.sqlite', '.sqlite3'}

Public symbols

ProjectUnderstandingService

Usage pattern

Import example Python 
from app.services.project_understanding_service import ProjectUnderstandingService

# Use ProjectUnderstandingService based on the module API.

Classes

ProjectUnderstandingService

class ProjectUnderstandingService:

Builds LLM-ready context and asks the selected provider to generate structured project understanding. Important: The LLM is used for narrative understanding only. Developer reference data such as API paths, handlers, signatures, parameters, return types, classes, and methods is merged from parser/analyzer output to avoid hallucination.

Attributes

Name Type Default / Value
REQUIRED_UNDERSTANDING_FIELDS - {'project_summary', 'architecture_overview', 'execution_flow', 'module_responsibilities', 'api_overview', 'key_dependencies', 'risks_and_gaps', 'recommended_document_sections'}

_generate_understanding_with_retry

def _generate_understanding_with_retry(self, provider: Any, user_prompt: str) -> dict[str, Any]:

Generate project understanding from the LLM. The first attempt uses the normal prompt. If the LLM returns malformed JSON or misses required fields, retry once with stricter JSON-only instructions.

Calls made by this function

Called function Call details Why it matters

_merge_developer_reference_data

def _merge_developer_reference_data(self, understanding: dict[str, Any], project_context: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:

Merge reliable parser/analyzer data into LLM output. This makes final documentation closer to professional developer docs. API paths, handlers, signatures, classes, methods, and parameters should come from code parsing, not from LLM guesses.

app.services.runtime_context_analyzer

app/services/runtime_context_analyzer.py

`app.services.runtime_context_analyzer` contains 1 class(es) 0 sync function(s) 0 async function(s) 1 constant(s) 0 API route(s) and 2 internal dependency link(s).

Public symbols

RUNTIME_CONTEXT_SYSTEM_PROMPTRuntimeContextAnalyzer

Usage pattern

Import example Python 
from app.services.runtime_context_analyzer import RUNTIME_CONTEXT_SYSTEM_PROMPT

# Use RUNTIME_CONTEXT_SYSTEM_PROMPT based on the module API.

Constants

Name Type Value
RUNTIME_CONTEXT_SYSTEM_PROMPT - '\nYou are AutoDocX Runtime Context Analyzer.\n\nYour job is to analyze a software project using:\n1. Parsed project/code scan context.\n2. User-written runtime/tooling notes.\n3. Screenshots attached by the user.\n\nYou must create practical developer-facing runtime documentation.\n\nImportant:\n- Do not assume the project is Python.\n- Do not assume the project uses FastAPI, Streamlit, Docker, Temporal, Swagger, or any specific tool.\n- Detect the actual language, framework, runtime, commands, ports, services, dashboards, and workflow from the available evidence.\n- If the code scanner provides limited details for a language such as Java, C, C++, Go, JavaScript, TypeScript, or another stack, use filenames, folder names, config files, build files, commands, screenshots, and user notes as supporting evidence.\n- Separate confirmed facts from reasonable inferences.\n- Do not invent tools, services, APIs, databases, or cloud systems that are not supported by code, screenshots, logs, filenames, or user notes.\n\nYou must explain:\n- What the project appears to be.\n- How a developer runs it.\n- What tools or services are involved.\n- How the UI, backend, APIs, workers, jobs, workflows, scripts, or external systems interact.\n- What each screenshot proves.\n- What operational risks, missing setup steps, or unclear areas remain.\n\nUse screenshots as evidence, not decoration.\nUse user notes as hints, but rewrite them into clear technical documentation.\nUse project scan context to connect runtime behavior to real files, modules, endpoints, scripts, commands, or configuration when possible.\n\nReturn only valid JSON.\nDo not use markdown.\nDo not wrap the response in ```json.\n\nReturn this exact JSON structure:\n\n{\n "runtime_summary": "string",\n "tooling_stack": [\n {\n "tool": "string",\n "purpose": "string",\n "evidence": "string"\n }\n ],\n "runtime_flow": [\n {\n "step": 1,\n "title": "string",\n "description": "string",\n "evidence": "string"\n }\n ],\n "screenshot_insights": [\n {\n "title": "string",\n "what_it_shows": "string",\n "why_it_matters": "string"\n }\n ],\n "operational_notes": [\n "string"\n ],\n "risks_or_gaps": [\n {\n "title": "string",\n "description": "string",\n "severity": "low|medium|high"\n }\n ]\n}\n\nWriting rules:\n- Be specific when evidence is clear.\n- Be cautious when evidence is weak.\n- Say "not visible from the provided context" when something is unknown.\n- Do not overfit the explanation to the screenshot only; connect it with the code scan.\n- Do not mention that you are an AI model.\n- Do not include generic filler.\n- Keep the output useful for a developer joining the project.\n'

Classes

RuntimeContextAnalyzer

class RuntimeContextAnalyzer:

Uses a vision-capable LLM to analyze project code context, user-written runtime notes, and screenshots together. This turns pasted screenshots into real project understanding, instead of simply placing raw screenshots into the generated docs.

Attributes

Name Type Default / Value
REQUIRED_FIELDS - {'runtime_summary', 'tooling_stack', 'runtime_flow', 'screenshot_insights', 'operational_notes', 'risks_or_gaps'}

app.services.scan_comparator

app/services/scan_comparator.py

`app.services.scan_comparator` contains 1 class(es) 0 sync function(s) 0 async function(s) 0 constant(s) 0 API route(s) and 0 internal dependency link(s).

Public symbols

ScanComparator

Usage pattern

Import example Python 
from app.services.scan_comparator import ScanComparator

# Use ScanComparator based on the module API.

Classes

ScanComparator

class ScanComparator:

Compares two stored AutoDocX project scans. The comparator detects: • added files • deleted files • modified files • added, removed and modified functions • added, removed and modified classes • added, removed and modified methods • added and removed routes • added and removed internal dependencies

Attributes

Name Type Default / Value
IGNORED_COMPARISON_FIELDS - {'line', 'end_line'}

Methods

ScanStorage

class ScanStorage:

Stores and retrieves AutoDocX project scan results. Scans are saved under: workspace/scans/<project_name>/<scan_id>.json

Methods

build_context_blocks_payload

def build_context_blocks_payload(context_blocks: list[dict[str, Any]]) -> tuple[str, list[tuple[str, tuple[str, bytes, str]]]]:

Build multipart form payload for ordered context blocks. The screenshots are sent as files. Each block stores screenshot_index so the backend can connect the correct image to the correct block.

Execution Flow

1

Scan project files

ProjectScanner walks the provided project_path, applies ignore rules, computes file hashes, and parses Python files using PythonCodeParser to extract imports, functions, classes, constants, decorators, and FastAPI routes.

app.services.project_scannerapp.services.code_parser
2

Build project analysis and context

ProjectAnalyzer aggregates parsed files into module maps, internal dependency lists, routes, and statistics. ProjectContextBuilder converts the analysis into a compact or detailed context used for LLM requests or downstream documentation generation.

app.services.project_analyzerapp.services.project_context_builder
3

Generate LLM understanding

ProjectUnderstandingService (or RuntimeContextAnalyzer for screenshots) selects an LLM provider via LLMProviderFactory, prepares the prompt using prompts builders, calls the provider, retries once on malformed responses, and validates required fields in the returned JSON.

app.services.project_understanding_serviceapp.services.runtime_context_analyzerapp.services.llm.factoryapp.services.llm.prompts
4

Merge developer reference data

LLM-produced narrative is merged with deterministic parser/analyzer output (API reference, module responsibilities, dependencies) to ensure signatures, routes, and symbols come from code rather than LLM inference.

app.services.project_understanding_serviceapp.services.project_context_builder
5

Build and save HTML documentation

DocumentBuilder renders a standalone professional HTML document from the merged understanding and runtime context. DocumentStorage saves HTML, metadata, and optional runtime assets (screenshots) under the configured workspace/documents path.

app.services.document_builderapp.services.document_storage
6

Expose results and history via API/UI

FastAPI routes orchestrate requests (scan, understand, generate, sync, compare) and return structured Pydantic responses. The Streamlit UI (ui.streamlit_app) calls these endpoints to present results, allow uploads of runtime context blocks and screenshots, and open generated HTML documents.

app.api.project_routesapp.mainui.streamlit_app

Troubleshooting and Production Notes

high

No API authentication on endpoints

The exposed FastAPI routes perform project scans, LLM calls, and write files but the provided context does not show any authentication, authorization, or rate-limiting. This may allow unauthorized use and accidental exposure of LLM API keys or workspace files.

high

LLM API key and provider configuration surface

LLM provider, API key and model selection are read from app.core.config and passed around; the scanning output does not show secrets management (vault, KMS) or per-request key isolation. Secure storage and rotation of keys and role-based access for who can trigger LLM calls are not described.

medium

Partial observability and monitoring

The code includes retries for LLM calls but there is no explicit telemetry, structured logging, or metrics export shown. Production readiness would benefit from request/operation tracing, error metrics, and alerting for failed scans or failed LLM calls.

medium

Persistence durability and backups

Scans, understandings, and documents are saved to a workspace directory. There is no mention of durable remote storage, retention policies, or backup strategies for the workspace artifacts.

medium

Limited input validation for uploaded images

DocumentStorage and Streamlit paths accept uploaded screenshots and recover embedded base64 images, but input size/content validation and virus/malware scanning are not evident. Asset handling should restrict types, size, and scan for unsafe content.

medium

Testing and CI coverage not visible

The scan does not expose unit or integration tests, test harnesses, or CI configuration. Critical components—parsing, comparator, LLM response parsing and retry logic—would benefit from deterministic tests and mocks for LLM providers.
